author: @bazzatron9482
text:
Hey Luke, you can get a slightly higher amount of strength by still using screws! Instead of merely countersinking the screw, counter-bore down about 5-10mm, sink the head of the screw below the surface of your top board, then fill in the hole with the dowel just as you did here.

Using the counter-bore method, you can get the same look, but with better holding power, and you can carry on working the piece without having to wait for glue to dry - great for production!

Also agree with comments about using a thin-kerf saw like a razor saw. I prefer the Japanese style saws, as their thin plate and low set make cutting easy without marking the surface of your board.

Good tip! Would love to hear how you drilled your holes nice and square!

author: @htmagic
text: Luke, I use a flush cut razor saw to cut off the excess. Saves time in sanding since it is mostly flush after cutting.

author: @Burnintrees384
text: Making hardwood dowels of a very different color from your main wood looks fantastic. Purple Heart and padouk are my favorite combo.

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: I do love contrasting colours in wood, oak and pine are one of my favourite combos. I must give it a try with dowels. Thanks mate! :)

author: @TerryPullen
text: Doweling like this let's you dry fit prior to glue-up if you need to.  Also consider a Miller dowel, similar but different.

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: Thanks for the tip, It looks good I might have to get some for some later wood work projects. :)
